text:
Titre:
Catalyst Fund' s $30m 2nd close deepens bet on Africa's climate resilience founders


Résumé:
Pan-African venture fund Catalyst Fund announced a $30 million second close, bringing total capital closer to its target. The fund backs pre-seed to Series A startups building climate resilience solutions for underserved communities. New investors include IFC, FASA, Shell Foundation, Trafigura Foundation, Speedinvest, BlinkCV, and high-net-worth individuals, joining earlier backers FSD Africa and Cisco Foundation. Catalyst Fund expects final close later in 2026.

Article:
Catalyst Fund, the pan-African venture fund and venture builder backing founders from pre-seed to Series A, has announced a US$30 million second close, bringing it closer to its target and adding fresh momentum to its mission of strengthening climate resilience across the continent. Catalyst Fund is a pre-seed VC fund and accelerator backing high-impact tech startups that seek to improve the resilience of underserved, climate-vulnerable communities. It partners with mission-driven founders that share our vision of a world where every individual has the tools and opportunities they need to thrive

text:
Titre:
African Payment Providers Bypass SWIFT for Stablecoin Settlement


Résumé:
African payment service providers (PSPs) are increasingly adopting stablecoin settlement via USDC and USDT to bypass SWIFT's slow, costly correspondent banking system. Swiss-based institutional OTC desk FinchTrade facilitates this shift, offering same-day settlement, reduced FX exposure, and predictable fees. The article highlights a $1M Nigeria-to-Germany payment costing $5,000-$8,000 via stablecoins versus $40,000-$70,000 via SWIFT, with settlement compressed from days to hours. Regulatory frameworks in Nigeria and South Africa are evolving to support this infrastructure, with expansion expected to more African corridors.


Startups mentionnées:
FinchTrade
Fireblocks


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Aucune


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
For African PSPs and EMIs handling cross-border B2B settlement, correspondent banking has become a structural drag. SWIFT settlement windows run T+3 to T+5. Cumulative fees hit 3% to 7% across intermediary banks. Working capital sits tied up in days-long FX exposure. These are now line items PSPs measure against alternatives that didn't exist 18 months ago. FinchTrade, a Swiss-based institutional OTC desk, sees this shift daily

metadata.title: African Payment Providers Bypass SWIFT for Stablecoin Settlement

. FinchTrade, a Swiss-based institutional OTC desk, sees this shift daily. The company serves African payment companies as well as other international flows where stablecoin settlement through USDC and USDT has become an operational default."For our PSP clients, this is no longer about offering crypto products to end users," says Nicola Boldrini, growth lead at FinchTrade."It's about whether their cross-border settlement actually settles the same day or takes a week." Three operational realities are driving the migration: settlement reliability, FX exposure compression, and cost predictability

metadata.title: African Payment Providers Bypass SWIFT for Stablecoin Settlement

A Nigerian importer paying a German supplier $1 million faces compounding differences: SWIFT settlement typically costs $40,000 to $70,000 with four to five days of FX volatility, while the same payment routed through Naira-to-USDC-to-Euro clears within hours at $5,000 to $8,000 total cost. FinchTrade provides the crypto-fiat conversion layer and connects African PSPs to global stablecoin liquidity, using regulated stablecoin issuers, institutional custody (Fireblocks), and compliance platforms

FinchTrade holds a Swiss license through VQF and has processed over $1 billion in client trading volume since launch, running corridor settlement across Nigerian naira, Ghanaian cedi, Kenyan shilling, and South African rand. The business impact includes settlement time compressed from days to hours, all-in costs reduced by an order of magnitude, and working capital released from in-flight FX exposure. The shift also opens corridors that correspondent banking has historically underserved. Regulatory frameworks are maturing: the Central Bank of Nigeria has indicated openness to stablecoin payment infrastructure in B2B contexts, and South Africa's SARB framework provides workable guidelines

BrainsMingle, which has already attracted professionals and communities from more than 90 countries, has announced a US$400,000 strategic seed investment from BasharSoft Group, Egypt's leading human capital technology group, which operates WUZZUF and Forasna. This partnership represents BasharSoft's first strategic investment since its acquisition of iCareer last year

Article:
Cascador, an Africa-focused platform for growth-stage founders building businesses with social impact, has deployed over US$5 million into seven innovative startups at its second annual pitch day. Disrupt Africa reported last March that Cascador had announced the launch of its Catalytic Fund, a US$2 million annual initiative providing tailored financial support to Cascador alumni companies. Since its founding in 2019, Cascador has supported over 60 entrepreneurs who have collectively raised more than US$55 million in capital

text:
Titre:
Stablecoins Are Not Killing the Naira. They Are Keeping Nigerian Businesses Alive


Résumé:
The article argues that stablecoins like USDT and USDC are not undermining the Nigerian naira but are instead providing essential financial infrastructure for businesses and freelancers to manage currency volatility, preserve value, and facilitate international payments. It highlights Nigeria's $92.1 billion in crypto transactions in 2024, driven by utility, and mentions Monica.Cash as a platform bridging stablecoin-to-naira conversion.


Startups mentionnées:
Monica Cash
Monica.Cash


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Aucune


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
The conversation around stablecoins in Nigeria often starts from the wrong premise, writes Mbah Casmir, founder and CEO of Monica Cash, a cryptocurrency-to-naira exchange platform helping individuals and businesses across Nigeria convert digital assets seamlessly. For years, digital assets, particularly dollar-backed stablecoins such as USDT and USDC, have been framed as threats to the naira. Critics argue that their growing adoption encourages capital flight, weakens confidence in the local currency, and accelerates dollarisation. While those concerns may sound reasonable, they overlook what is actually happening across Nigeria' s economy

metadata.title: Stablecoins Are Not Killing the Naira. They Are Keeping Nigerian Businesses Alive

text:
Titre:
Stablecoins Are Not Killing the Naira. They Are Keeping Nigerian Businesses Alive


Résumé:
The article argues that stablecoins like USDT and USDC are not undermining the Nigerian naira but are instead providing essential financial infrastructure for businesses and freelancers to manage currency volatility, preserve value, and facilitate international payments. It highlights Nigeria's $92.1 billion in crypto transactions in 2024, driven by utility, and mentions Monica.Cash as a platform bridging stablecoin-to-naira conversion.


Startups mentionnées:
Monica Cash
Monica.Cash


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Aucune


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
. The reality is that most Nigerians are not turning to stablecoins because they want to speculate on cryptocurrency. They are turning to them because they are looking for practical ways to navigate economic uncertainty, preserve value, and keep businesses running. For thousands of small businesses, importers, exporters, freelancers, and remote workers, stablecoins have quietly become part of everyday financial planning. Consider the experience of a small importer sourcing products from suppliers abroad. A transaction that looks profitable on Monday can easily become a loss before payment is finally settled

metadata.title: Stablecoins Are Not Killing the Naira. They Are Keeping Nigerian Businesses Alive

text:
Titre:
Stablecoins Are Not Killing the Naira. They Are Keeping Nigerian Businesses Alive


Résumé:
The article argues that stablecoins like USDT and USDC are not undermining the Nigerian naira but are instead providing essential financial infrastructure for businesses and freelancers to manage currency volatility, preserve value, and facilitate international payments. Pays:

Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
. For many businesses operating on tight margins, that kind of volatility makes it extremely difficult to plan with confidence. Every delay creates uncertainty, and every exchange rate movement can directly affect profitability. Stablecoins have become one of the tools helping businesses manage that risk. Rather than holding funds in a currency experiencing rapid fluctuations, many business owners temporarily preserve value in digital dollars until transactions are completed. This is not an attempt to replace the naira. It is a response to the realities of operating in a highly volatile financial environment. The same pattern is playing out among Nigerian freelancers and remote professionals

metadata.title: Stablecoins Are Not Killing the Naira. They Are Keeping Nigerian Businesses Alive

Article:
. The same pattern is playing out among Nigerian freelancers and remote professionals. Nigeria has become one of Africa' s leading hubs for digital talent, with professionals increasingly working for clients across North America, Europe, and other international markets. Yet receiving and managing international payments through traditional channels can still involve delays, friction, and conversion challenges. Stablecoins solves this problem by providing a faster and more flexible alternative that allows many professionals to exercise greater control over their earnings. This trend is not unique to Nigeria

metadata.title: Stablecoins Are Not Killing the Naira. They Are Keeping Nigerian Businesses Alive

. This trend is not unique to Nigeria. Across emerging markets experiencing currency pressures, stablecoins are increasingly functioning as financial infrastructure. They are helping individuals and businesses preserve purchasing power, and participate more effectively in the global economy. What makes Nigeria particularly remarkable is the scale of adoption. According to recent blockchain analytics data, Nigerians processed approximately $92.1 billion in cryptocurrency transactions in 2024, making the country one of the most active crypto markets globally

Article:
. A significant portion of that activity was driven by stablecoins, which tells an important story about how digital assets are being used in practice. Much of the demand is being driven by utility rather than speculation. The narrative that stablecoins are undermining the naira also overlooks a more important question: why are businesses turning to them in the first place? People generally adopt solutions that solve real problems. If stablecoin adoption continues to grow, it is because businesses and individuals are finding genuine value in the speed, accessibility, and financial flexibility these tools provide

That growing reliance on stablecoins is also why companies like Monica.Cash have emerged, because the challenge for many users is no longer accessing stablecoins, but converting them quickly, securely, and efficiently whenever business needs arise. Monica.Cash was built to help bridge that gap by making stablecoin-to-naira transactions faster, simpler, and more accessible for individuals and businesses that increasingly rely on digital assets as part of their financial activities

text:
Titre:
Nigeria's StableKlin is a behavioural intelligence platform designed for cleaning businesses


Résumé:
Nigerian startup StableKlin, founded by Dehinde Sogbanmu, offers a behavioural intelligence platform that helps cleaning businesses detect early signs of customer disengagement by analyzing booking patterns and behavioural changes. The self-funded startup is currently focused on the UK cleaning market and seeks pilot users to refine its dashboard and insights.


Startups mentionnées:
StableKlin


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Aucune


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
Nigerian startup StableKlin is a behavioural intelligence platform designed for cleaning businesses and other recurring service providers, which helps business owners understand something that is often difficult to see until it is too late - changes in customer behaviour. Most cleaning businesses already track bookings, customer details, invoices and payments."What they often do not have is a clear way to identify when a loyal customer is gradually becoming less engaged. Customers rarely announce that they are leaving. More often, they start booking less frequently, delay appointments, become less predictable, or quietly drift away over time," said Dehinde Sogbanmu, StableKlin' s founder

metadata.title: Nigeria's StableKlin is a behavioural intelligence platform designed for cleaning businesses

Article:
. StableKlin is built to detect those early warning signs. The platform analyses patterns such as a customer' s usual booking frequency, the time since their last service, changes in scheduling habits, consistency over time, and other behavioural indicators. It then highlights customers whose behaviour is beginning to move away from what has historically been normal for them."For example, if a customer typically books every four weeks but has not booked for three months, that may indicate more than a simple delay

. It could be an early sign that the relationship is weakening," said Sogbanmu."The goal is to give business owners greater visibility into customer stability so they can take action earlier, strengthen retention, and reduce the risk of losing valuable repeat business." StableKlin was born from a real-world problem Sogbanmu encountered while running Potteries Prime Cleaning, a cleaning business in the UK."As I worked with recurring customers, I noticed a pattern - customers rarely disappeared overnight. In most cases, there were subtle behavioural changes beforehand

Booking intervals became longer, routines became less consistent, and engagement gradually declined before revenue was affected," he said."I realised that while there were plenty of tools available to help manage operations, there were very few tools focused on understanding customer behaviour itself." That insight led him to begin developing StableKlin. Rather than focusing on operational management, the startup focuses on behavioural insight."It helps business owners identify changes in customer patterns before those changes become visible in revenue figures," said Sogbanmu

Titre:
How Zimbabwe's Maminda Agri-Fintech helps smallholders access finance, advisory, markets


Résumé:
Zimbabwean startup Maminda Agri-Fintech provides smallholder farmers with an integrated digital platform combining AI-powered farming support, digital financial services, satellite crop monitoring, cooperative financing, and market access tools. The bootstrapped startup is in pilot stage with fewer than 100 active users in Mashonaland and Manicaland, preparing for a pre-seed round and planning expansion to Zambia and Malawi.

Founded last year, Maminda Agri-Fintech combines AI-powered farming support, digital financial services, satellite crop monitoring, cooperative financing and market access tools designed specifically for underserved farming communities

. Many lack formal credit histories, limiting access to financing, quality inputs and profitable markets."Most existing solutions address only one challenge at a time, whether agronomic advisory, agricultural financing or market access. Maminda' s approach is to integrate these services into a single platform designed around the realities of smallholder farming," said Gandanzara. Competitors include agricultural financing and advisory platforms operating across Africa, such as Apollo Agriculture and other regional agri-fintech providers

How Zimbabwe's Maminda Agri-Fintech helps smallholders access finance, advisory, markets


Résumé:
Zimbabwean startup Maminda Agri-Fintech provides smallholder farmers with an integrated digital platform combining AI-powered farming support, digital financial services, satellite crop monitoring, cooperative financing, and market access tools. The bootstrapped startup is in pilot stage with fewer than 100 active users in Mashonaland and Manicaland, preparing for a pre-seed round and planning expansion to Zambia and Malawi.

Yet it is still at the pilot stage, working with a small group of farmers in Zimbabwe to validate product-market fit and refine the platform' s financial and agronomic models."The company currently has fewer than 100 active users

text:
Titre:
Pan-African WRAPPED helping people find jobs in film industry


Résumé:
WRAPPED, a pan-African platform founded by Zoe Ramushu, centralizes job hiring in the film and television industry, replacing fragmented WhatsApp groups and spreadsheets. The startup gained 600 sign-ups in its first days and secured a partnership with MultiChoice.


Startups mentionnées:
WRAPPED


Investisseurs mentionnés:
MultiChoice


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
"WRAPPED is the app that I wish I had when I was trying to break into this industry. Right now, hiring in film and television is done through WhatsApp groups, Facebook groups, spreadsheets, and institutional memory captured in human brains," WRAPPED founder Zoe Ramushu tells Disrupt Africa."A message drops in a WhatsApp group, a gig is gone in two minutes. You didn' t even know what that job was. Two minutes." Ramushu has come up with a solution. What WRAPPED does is give the industry one centralised place where everybody is, where all the jobs are getting booked out of, at the click of a button."We' re categorised by skills and experience level so you find exactly who you' re looking for

metadata.title: Pan-African WRAPPED helping people find jobs in film industry

text:
Titre:
Pan-African WRAPPED helping people find jobs in film industry


Résumé:
WRAPPED, a pan-African platform founded by Zoe Ramushu, centralizes job hiring in the film and television industry, replacing fragmented WhatsApp groups and spreadsheets. The startup gained 600 sign-ups in its first days and secured a partnership with MultiChoice.

Article:
. We cut down 33 per cent of the pre-production workload. It sounds simple and it is, that' s the innovation," Ramushu said. She said the idea for WRAPPED came up based on her own experiences."I co-founded Swift (Sisters Working in Film and Television) and spent five-to-six years voluntarily building that organisation because I believe in being part of the change I' d like to see. Then I got my first Netflix project. Close to 120 people on set. I said to my line producer, we need at least one female in every department. Silence in the room. Like I was joking. We found them, a female in every single department. But it was an exercise," said Ramushu."Time-consuming

. But it was an exercise," said Ramushu."Time-consuming. In production, time is literally money. That was the moment I said this cannot be the way things work. WRAPPED was real and existed in my mind from that moment. And glad today it' s now real for everyone else." The startup had 600 sign-ups in the first two to three days, with minimal marketing."That just spoke to the desire, the complete demand, for people working in this industry to have a centralised place to gather. A place that allows them to connect and be seen," said Ramushu."Our partnership with MultiChoice has been invaluable

Ramushu said she doesn' t see WRAPPED as an African platform."It is built by Africans but it is a global platform. If it' s good tech, it' s good tech. If it' s a good product, it' s a good product. Same as film. Our presence at Berlin, at Cannes, three years running, those invitations are evidence that we are global. That' s not a plan. That' s already happening," she said. text:
Titre:
Flat6Labs, IFC launch StartAlgeria capacity-building programme


Résumé:
Egypt's Flat6Labs, the International Finance Corporation (IFC), and Algeria's Ministry of Knowledge Economy, Startups and Micro-Enterprises have launched StartAlgeria, a capacity-building programme targeting entrepreneur support organisations (ESOs) in Algeria. The programme aims to strengthen ESOs' ability to support pre-seed and seed-stage startups through workshops, masterclasses, and six months of post-programme mentorship, focusing on startup selection, program design, investment readiness, fundraising strategy, and financial sustainability. The pilot cohort will focus on incubators in Algiers.

Article:
Egypt's Flat6Labs, the IFC and Algeria's Ministry of Knowledge Economy, Startups and Micro-Enterprises have launched StartAlgeria, a capacity-building programme that puts entrepreneur support organisations (ESOs) at the forefront of Algeria's ecosystem future. StartAlgeria comes at a key moment for Algeria's entrepreneurship landscape, shifting the focus toward improving how the ESOs operate by providing them with international best practices adapted to each organization's needs, a community-driven approach that focuses on peer learning, and facilitating connections with investors, policymakers, and key stakeholders

Katapult Africa opens applications for latest climate-focused accelerator programme


Résumé:
Katapult Africa has opened applications for its three-month climate-tech accelerator programme, offering equity investment of $150,000-$500,000, workshops, mentorship, and investor access to early-stage African startups in agritech, climate-smart agriculture, and resilient food systems.

The three-month Katapult Africa programme gives participants access to intensive workshops, learning sessions, and a broad network of key stakeholders, including investors and industry experts. This equips them with the tools, knowledge, and connections needed to scale their impact across the continent. Participating ventures will also receive between US$150,000 and US$500,000 in equity investment

text:
Titre:
African tech startups raise $260m in Q2 2026; down 40% on same period last year


Résumé:
Thirty-eight African tech startups raised $260 million in Q2 2026, a 40% decline from $427 million in Q2 2025. The annual African Tech Startups Funding Report by Disrupt Africa indicates a potential funding dip for 2026, with projected annual total of $1.28 billion, down from $1.64 billion in 2025.


Startups mentionnées:
Aucune


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Aucune


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
Thirty-eight African tech startups raised a total of US$260 million in the second quarter of 2026, down 40 per cent on the US$427 million raised in the same period of 2025. Total investment into the African tech startup ecosystem increased by almost 50 per cent to US$1.64 billion in 2025 as the sector began to slowly recover from the impacts of the global capital shortage, according to the 11th edition of the annual African Tech Startups Funding Report released by startup news and research portal Disrupt Africa. Numbers from Q1 2026 had suggested this year would at least match, and probably surpass, 2025, with 40 startups banking US$382,150,000 in funding

metadata.title: African tech startups raise $260m in Q2 2026; down 40% on same period last year

text:
Titre:
Nigeria's MyAgent launches to build trust level for African real estate


Résumé:
Nigerian prop-tech startup MyAgent has launched a platform connecting renters directly with verified landlords, aiming to build trust and transparency in Africa's real estate market, starting with Nigeria's rental sector. Founded by Samuel Afolabi, the startup is currently founder-funded, generating revenue through a 20% commission on rental transactions, and is now fundraising to accelerate supply-side growth.


Startups mentionnées:
MyAgent


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Aucune


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
Nigerian startup MyAgent has launched a prop-tech platform it says is building the trust and transaction layer for real estate in Africa, starting with Nigeria's rental market. Launched earlier this year by Samuel Afolabi, MyAgent connects renters directly with verified landlords, reduces dependence on agents, and makes the rental process faster, more transparent, and less expensive."The long-term vision is to become the default infrastructure for property transactions, covering rentals first, then land, sales, shortlets, and eventually the data layer for the market," Afolabi told Disrupt Africa

Titre:
LemFi Acquires Wealth8 to Expand into Wealth Management and Investment Services


Résumé:
LemFi, a cross-border financial platform, has received UK FCA approval to acquire investment platform Wealth8, enabling it to offer wealth-building and investment services to its 2 million+ customers. The acquisition marks LemFi's entry into wealthtech and its evolution into a full financial-life platform for diaspora communities.


Startups mentionnées:
LemFi
Wealth8


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Highland Europe


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
LemFi, the financial platform for people living and working across borders, has secured approval from the UK's Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) for its acquisition of investment platform Wealth8. The approval paves the way for LemFi to add wealth-building and investment to its product ecosystem, marking the company's entry into wealthtech and a significant step in its evolution into a full financial-life platform. LemFi is the trusted platform helping people move money across borders, access credit, and save money across the UK, Europe, North America and Australia. The Wealth8 acquisition adds a missing piece: the ability to grow money over the long term

metadata.title: LemFi Acquires Wealth8 to Expand into Wealth Management and Investment Services

. The Wealth8 acquisition adds a missing piece: the ability to grow money over the long term. It continues an arc familiar to millions of people: build life in a new country, support family back home, save, establish a credit footprint, and ultimately invest in a more secure future. With Wealth8, LemFi can now support that journey on a single platform. That momentum is already underway. In 2025, LemFi launched its Instant Access Savings Account, powered by ClearBank: a high-yield product that lets customers earn daily interest, paid monthly, on balances they can access at any time

. With promotional rates reaching 5.00% AER, it was a deliberate move to shift from just enabling customers to move money to helping customers keep and grow it. But Savings is only the first step in building lasting wealth; investing is also key, and it is precisely this crucial part of the financial ladder that newcomers are most excluded from. The opportunity is significant. While investing remains one of the UK's most powerful drivers of long-term wealth, participation remains uneven. Most UK adults with more than £10,000 in investible assets (61%) hold at least three-quarters of it in cash rather than investments. The barriers are sharpest for communities that have arrived from abroad

Research from the London School of Economics published in late 2025 found that the UK's ethnic wealth gap has widened over the past decade. Runnymede Trust research revealed that for every £1 of wealth, several minority communities hold as little as 10-20p. The gap reflects unequal income, but also access to the assets that compound into wealth over time. Wealth8 was founded to address exactly this divide, with a mission to make investing simple, affordable, and accessible to communities that mainstream platforms have overlooked, offering minimums as low as £8 and diversified portfolios

Article:
Tunisian startup RoboCare, founded in Sfax, develops an agricultural management platform that leverages satellite imagery, drone data, IoT sensors, weather data, and field expertise to help farmers detect crop diseases early, optimize resource usage, and improve farm performance. The company has secured a six-figure funding round from 216 Capital to support its next growth phase and expansion into African and Middle Eastern markets. text:
Titre:
Kenyan startups Fuzu, Kyosk secure funding from Jobtech Alliance


Résumé:
Kenyan startups Fuzu and Kyosk have secured undisclosed funding from the Jobtech Alliance, an ecosystem-building initiative steered by Mercy Corps and BFA Global. Fuzu, a talent infrastructure platform pivoting to managed AI data operations, and Kyosk, a B2B commerce platform serving over 200,000 informal retailers across East Africa, are the latest additions to the alliance's portfolio.

Fuzu
Kyosk


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Jobtech Alliance

Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
Kenyan startups Fuzu and Kyosk are the latest to secure an undisclosed amount of funding from the Jobtech Alliance. Disrupt Africa reported in October 2022 on the launch of the Jobtech Alliance, an ecosystem-building initiative around inclusive jobtech in Africa steered by Mercy Corps and BFA Global, which aims to create an enabling environment for entrepreneurs to build platforms that deliver quality livelihoods, are inclusive, and enable users to engage in decent work. It has run several cohorts, but is now also making direct investments in relevant ventures, most recently Nigeria's Bumpa and Kenya's Flowcart, both e-commerce ventures, and Kenyan social commerce startup Twiva

Titre:
BioNTech to Acquire InstaDeep to Strengthen Pioneering Position in the Field of AI-powered Drug Discovery, Design and Development


Résumé:
BioNTech announced an agreement to acquire InstaDeep, a global AI and machine learning technology company, for approximately £362 million upfront plus up to £200 million in performance-based milestones. The acquisition aims to enhance BioNTech's AI-driven drug discovery capabilities and add around 240 skilled professionals to its workforce.


Startups mentionnées:
InstaDeep


Investisseurs mentionnés:
BioNTech


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Tunisia


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
MAINZ, Germany, and LONDON, United Kingdom, January 09, 2023 - BioNTech SE (Nasdaq: BNTX,"BioNTech") and InstaDeep Ltd. ("InstaDeep") today announced that they have entered into an agreement under which BioNTech will acquire InstaDeep, a leading global technology company in the field of artificial intelligence ("AI") and machine learning ("ML"). The transaction includes a total upfront consideration of approximately £362 million in cash and BioNTech shares to acquire 100% of the remaining InstaDeep shares, excluding the shares already owned by BioNTech

metadata.title: BioNTech to Acquire InstaDeep to Strengthen Pioneering Position in the Field of AI-powered Drug Discovery, Design and Development

The transaction follows BioNTech' s initial equity investment as part of InstaDeep' s Series B financing round in January 2022. The acquisition supports BioNTech' s strategy to build world-leading capabilities in AI-driven drug discovery and development of next-generation immunotherapies and vaccines to address diseases with high unmet medical need

text:
Titre:
How Akuna Wallet, Trenderz want to fix creator payments in Africa


Résumé:
Africa's creator economy faces significant payment infrastructure gaps, excluding millions of Francophone African creators from platform monetization. Startups like Akuna Wallet, Trenderz, and Mainstack are building payment infrastructure to enable cross-border payouts, standardized contracts, and reliable payment systems for creators in countries like Côte d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Benin, and DRC.


Startups mentionnées:
Akuna Wallet
Trenderz
Mainstack


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Aucune


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
Africa's creator economy has a payment problem. These startups want to fix it. By Lina Kacyem, 2nd Jun, 2026. Africa hosts millions of digital content creators, a booming social media landscape, and one of the planet's youngest populations. Yet financial infrastructure to pay them fairly is virtually non-existent. The creator economy on the continent is expanding rapidly. According to the International School of Advertising, the creator economy market was valued between $3 and $5.1 billion in 2025 and is projected to reach $29.84 billion by 2032, with a CAGR of 28.7%. Smartphones are proliferating rapidly

metadata.title: How Akuna Wallet, Trenderz want to fix creator payments in Africa

text:
Titre:
How Akuna Wallet, Trenderz want to fix creator payments in Africa


Résumé:
Africa's creator economy faces significant payment infrastructure gaps, excluding millions of Francophone African creators from platform monetization. Startups like Akuna Wallet, Trenderz, and Mainstack are building payment infrastructure to enable cross-border payouts, standardized contracts, and reliable payment systems for creators in countries like Côte d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Benin, and DRC.


Startups mentionnées:
Akuna Wallet
Trenderz
Mainstack


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Aucune


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
. Smartphones are proliferating rapidly. TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube audiences are growing faster in Africa than anywhere else. Yet for the vast majority of African creators - particularly those in Francophone West Africa - one question remains unanswered: how to get paid? The most visible expression of this gap is the exclusion of most African countries from direct monetization programs on platforms where creators build their livelihoods. In 2026, the YouTube Partner Program is available in over 120 countries, but eligibility is not universal - creators must reside in a supported region to access monetization

metadata.title: How Akuna Wallet, Trenderz want to fix creator payments in Africa

text:
Titre:
How Akuna Wallet, Trenderz want to fix creator payments in Africa


Résumé:
Africa's creator economy faces significant payment infrastructure gaps, excluding millions of Francophone African creators from platform monetization. Startups like Akuna Wallet, Trenderz, and Mainstack are building payment infrastructure to enable cross-border payouts, standardized contracts, and reliable payment systems for creators in countries like Côte d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Benin, and DRC.


Startups mentionnées:
Akuna Wallet
Trenderz
Mainstack


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Aucune


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
. For creators in Côte d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Benin, or the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) - countries with tens of millions of social media users - YouTube ad revenue remains structurally inaccessible through conventional channels. Reasons accumulate: global platforms offering monetization often require an international bank account or do not accept local banks for payouts. PayPal coverage is partial or non-existent. Stripe remains unavailable in most Francophone African markets. A creator with 500,000 subscribers in Abidjan, building audiences for international brands, still cannot receive a YouTube payout directly to a local bank account

metadata.title: How Akuna Wallet, Trenderz want to fix creator payments in Africa

text:
Titre:
How Akuna Wallet, Trenderz want to fix creator payments in Africa


Résumé:
Africa's creator economy faces significant payment infrastructure gaps, excluding millions of Francophone African creators from platform monetization. Startups like Akuna Wallet, Trenderz, and Mainstack are building payment infrastructure to enable cross-border payouts, standardized contracts, and reliable payment systems for creators in countries like Côte d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Benin, and DRC.


Startups mentionnées:
Akuna Wallet
Trenderz
Mainstack


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Aucune


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
. Although Africa represents 18% of the world's population, the continent captures only about 5.4% of global content creation revenue - a gap explained not by production volume or audience engagement, but by payment infrastructure failures. The problem extends beyond platform payouts. Influencer marketing in the region operates through a fragmented network of WhatsApp negotiations, cash payments, in-kind arrangements - dinners and hotel stays as fees - and informal one-off transfers that can take weeks to execute. There are no standardized contracts, no performance tracking infrastructure, no reliable cross-border payment system

Spotify Bets On AI Podcasts, Smarter Ads, And Fan Experiences At 2026 Investor Day


Résumé:
At its 2026 Investor Day, Spotify outlined a strategy to become an all-in-one media and AI powerhouse, centered on its"Large Taste Model" that processes 3.4 trillion daily taste signals. Key announcements included AI-generated personal podcasts, adaptive fitness-guided audio sessions, a"Reserved" feature for early concert ticket access, interactive podcast Q&A for Premium users, paid podcast memberships, expansion of its audiobook catalog to 700,000+ titles, and a rebuilt advertising infrastructure driving 68% year-over-year advertiser growth.

Article:
If you still think of Spotify as just a place to stream your favorite playlists, think again. At its 2026 Investor Day, the company dropped a massive hint about its future, making it clear they are officially moving past the"music app" label. Instead, Spotify is aiming for something much bigger: an all-in-one media and AI powerhouse fueled by hyper-personalization, creator tools, and your unique taste. At the center of that strategy is what Spotify calls its"Large Taste Model," an AI system trained on billions of user interactions across music, podcasts, and audiobooks

The shift is already showing up in product announcements. Spotify introduced AI-generated"Personal Podcasts," audio programs built around user prompts and listening habits. It also previewed fitness-guided sessions that automatically adapt music tempo and coaching cues to match a workout pace. Another upcoming feature, called"Reserved," will allow top fans to access concert tickets before public sale through partnerships tied to artist engagement data

What stood out was how aggressively Spotify framed AI as part of the listening experience itself rather than a background recommendation engine. Cons: Steep learning curve; not truly hands-off; stocks only. 3. 3Commas - Best for Multi-Exchange Bot Trading: Best for users comfortable configuring their own bots. 3Commas offers grid, DCA, and other bot types across many exchanges. Setup took longer in our test because meaningful results depend on how you configure it - powerful, but not plug-and-play. Its risk behavior is only as good as your settings, so it rewards users willing to tune parameters

Titre:
217+ startups, ₦11B fraud, and $3B valuations: Insights from the 2025 Nigeria Payments Report


Résumé:
Zone and TechCabal Insights released the 2025 Nigeria Payments Report, highlighting explosive growth in e-payment channels (70% YoY transaction volume rise), regulatory momentum from CBN initiatives, cross-border expansion by fintechs like LemFi and Rise leveraging $20B+ remittances, and persistent challenges including infrastructure gaps, fraud threats, and a 54% dip in African fintech funding. Key players: Moniepoint adapted to stricter KYC, Flutterwave valued at $3B despite ₦11B fraud incident, Zone processes ₦100B monthly. VCs invested $140M in H1 2024. Future focus: AI fraud detection, open banking, AfCFTA cross-border payments, bridging funding gap.

Article:
Zone, a regulated blockchain-powered payment infrastructure company, has released the Nigeria Payments Report 2025 in partnership with TechCabal Insights. This second edition builds on the 2024 report which tracked how COVID-19 spurred a surge in digital payments, with e-transaction values doubling to over ₦1.25 quadrillion, and over 200 fintech startups accounting for 42% of all tech funding, driving financial inclusion to 76%. The 2025 report maps Nigeria's fast-evolving payment landscape, covering open banking, AI-powered fraud detection, and blockchain innovations. Standout players and milestones: Moniepoint adapted quickly to stricter KYC rules while deepening mobile money adoption

Titre:
New GSMA report shows how Africa's top mobile operators are building AI language models in African languages


Résumé:
The GSMA Mobile Economy Africa 2026 report reveals that six major African mobile operators (Airtel, Axian Telecom, Ethio Telecom, MTN, Orange, Vodacom) have partnered to build AI language models trained on African languages. The initiative has already produced a working Swahili reasoning model and aims to address barriers in data, compute, talent, and policy to unlock AI for Africa's 2,000+ languages.

Zindi

Article:
GSMA published its Mobile Economy Africa 2026 report on Tuesday, and one section details how six of Africa's largest mobile network operators have partnered with the industry body to build artificial intelligence (AI) language models trained on African languages. The effort is aimed at closing a gap that leaves most of the continent unable to use mainstream AI tools in the languages people actually speak

metadata.title: New GSMA report shows how Africa's top mobile operators are building AI language models in African languages

Article:
. Known as the G6, the operators - Airtel, Axian Telecom, Ethio Telecom, MTN, Orange, and Vodacom - first unveiled the initiative at Mobile World Congress (MWC) Kigali in October 2025, under the banner"AI language models in Africa, by Africa, for Africa." The collaboration brings together operators, researchers, startups, and civil society groups to tackle four barriers standing in the way of African-led AI development: data, compute, talent, and policy. The stakes are significant. Africa is home to more than 2,000 languages, accounting for over 30% of the world's total, yet large language models (LLMs) are trained mostly on English and other high-resource languages

. That leaves hundreds of millions of people unable to use AI tools by voice or text in their own languages. The initiative has already produced a tangible result. At MWC Barcelona 2026, developers demonstrated the first open Swahili reasoning model, built in partnership with MeetKai Zambia. The model can browse and translate online content in Swahili, offering a template that could be replicated for other African languages. Closing this language gap is not only a social good, the report argues, it carries real economic weight

On skills, the MTN Skills Academy, a zero-rated platform open across 16 African markets, is training the workforce AI rollouts will need. Released in February 2026 after three years of work with African universities, including Makerere University, the University of Ghana, and Addis Ababa University, the open dataset captures speech across 27 Sub-Saharan African languages spoken by more than 100 million people, designed to train voice AI and speech-recognition tools. Separately, the GSMA teamed up with Zindi, an African data science platform, to launch the African Trust & Safety LLM Challenge in March 2026. The result is a reusable safety benchmark that tests how AI models perform across more than 2,000 of the continent's languages.

Titre:
Nigeria sets October date for a digital postcode plan first proposed in 2009


Résumé:
Nigeria will launch the first phase of a new digital postcode system in October, assigning every building a unique, machine-readable digital address tied to precise map coordinates. The National Digital Alphanumeric Postcode System aims to replace descriptive, landmark-based addresses that have complicated deliveries, identity verification, and emergency response. The plan, first proposed in 2009, has seen multiple false starts, but recent Federal Executive Council approval and validation exercises suggest this attempt may succeed, with adoption being the key challenge.

. He described it as one of five defining initiatives from his three years in office, alongside Project BRIDGE, the country' s 90,000-kilometre fibre rollout; the National Universal Communication Access Project, which is extending connectivity to more than 20 million Nigerians through 3,700 telecommunications towers; the Nigeria Data Exchange; and ongoing efforts to build out an artificial intelligence ecosystem.Two decades of false starts: Nigeria has run a six-digit numeric postcode (Lagos Island' s is 100001) since 1986, but missing street names and house numbers across much of the country have limited its usefulness

metadata.title: Nigeria sets October date for a digital postcode plan first proposed in 2009

text:
Titre:
Nigeria sets October date for a digital postcode plan first proposed in 2009


Résumé:
Nigeria will launch the first phase of a new digital postcode system in October, assigning every building a unique, machine-readable digital address tied to precise map coordinates. The National Digital Alphanumeric Postcode System aims to replace descriptive, landmark-based addresses that have complicated deliveries, identity verification, and emergency response. The plan, first proposed in 2009, has seen multiple false starts, but recent Federal Executive Council approval and validation exercises suggest this attempt may succeed, with adoption being the key challenge.


Startups mentionnées:
what3words


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Aucune


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
. A plan to digitise it first surfaced in 2009, under the late President Umaru Musa Yar' Adua, before stalling amid policy and leadership changes. NIPOST tried smaller fixes: in 2017, it partnered with the British startup what3words to simplify location descriptions, and in 2018 launched two verification platforms, the Address Verification System and the Digital Addressing System, meant to build a live database of physical addresses

metadata.title: Nigeria sets October date for a digital postcode plan first proposed in 2009

text:
Titre:
Nigeria sets October date for a digital postcode plan first proposed in 2009


Résumé:
Nigeria will launch the first phase of a new digital postcode system in October, assigning every building a unique, machine-readable digital address tied to precise map coordinates. The National Digital Alphanumeric Postcode System aims to replace descriptive, landmark-based addresses that have complicated deliveries, identity verification, and emergency response. The plan, first proposed in 2009, has seen multiple false starts, but recent Federal Executive Council approval and validation exercises suggest this attempt may succeed, with adoption being the key challenge.


Startups mentionnées:
what3words


Investisseurs mentionnés:

Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Aucun


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
. Neither solved the underlying problem, and by 2020, Google' s Plus Codes had entered the Nigerian market with a similar workaround.What is different this time: In March, the Federal Executive Council approved a Geographic Information System (GIS)-enabled version of the postcode, and NIPOST has spent recent months running a delineation model validation exercise, checking aerially mapped postcode boundaries against ground realities. October' s rollout will cover a pilot group of states and local governments, not the whole country at once

metadata.title: Nigeria sets October date for a digital postcode plan first proposed in 2009

. NIPOST' s Postmaster General and Chief Executive Officer, Tola Odeyemi, framed the bigger risk as adoption rather than engineering: implementation, not technology, will determine whether the system succeeds, she said at the workshop, adding that the goal now is to move from awareness to use.

Article:
Disrup Tech Ventures, a leading early-stage fintech venture capital fund, announces its investment in Chari, a Y Combinator-backed startup that is transforming Morocco' s retail sector into a nationwide fintech network. This marks Disrup Tech' s first investment in Morocco and its second in Africa outside of Egypt, underscoring the fund' s growing commitment to supporting fintech innovation across the continent. Founded in 2020 by Ismael Belkhayat and Sophia Alj, Chari has built a licensed fintech platform that turns thousands of neighbourhood shops into access points for digital payments and financial services

The company is digitising financial flows in the informal economy, reducing dependency on cash, providing instant working capital, and embedding insurance and payment solutions directly into merchants' day-to-day operations

Lofty Inc Capital Management has secured a $43 million first close for its new Lofty Inc Alpha Fund, a late-seed investment vehicle aimed at scaling Africa' s most promising tech startups, particularly in Nigeria, Egypt, Kenya, and Francophone Africa. Lofty Inc is one of Africa' s most established venture capital firms. With over a decade of investing experience, the firm has backed startups such as Flutterwave, Andela, Wave Mobile Money, Omni Retail, Relianc Health, Thndr, and Moove-which recently secured $100 million in investment from Uber and Mubadala. Lofty Inc has played a key role in shaping Africa' s tech ecosystem while delivering strong returns to investors

. With modular integration, pay-as-you-go pricing, and a user-friendly interface, the startup enables even the smallest businesses to implement robust verification systems without compromising on quality or compliance."Having The Baobab Network on board is a game-changer," said co-founder and CEO, Aoussar."We' re building a future where verifying your identity is seamless, secure, and accessible-regardless of business size or geography." With new funding and growing momentum, VOVE ID is positioning itself as a vital player in the digital identity infrastructure for emerging markets.

metadata.title: Morocco's VOVE ID secures fresh funds to support expansion ambitions

First Circle Capital, a specialist early-stage venture fund focused on African fintech innovation, has secured US$6 million from the International Finance Corporation (IFC), a member of the World Bank Group, to expand its investments in high-potential financial technology startups across the continent. With offices in Casablanca and Kampala, First Circle Capital focuses on pre-seed and seed-stage companies solving foundational challenges in financial services. Kawarizmi brings deep programmatic expertise and international reach that will significantly accelerate our vision," said Noureddine Tayebi, CEO of Yassir. The acquisition will enable Yassir to build a scalable retail media network, monetise first-party data through privacy-first infrastructure, and expand international brand partnerships across Europe, MENA, and Africa

text:
Titre:
Wamda Capital leads Breez AI in $1.3 million pre-seed round


Résumé:
Breez AI has raised $1.3 million in pre-seed funding to scale its voice infrastructure system.


Startups mentionnées:
Breez AI


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Wamda Capital


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Morocco


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
Breez AI has raised $1.3 million in pre-seed funding to scale its voice infrastructure system. Built to make phone operations reliable, scalable, and natural-sounding, Breez received backing led by Wamda Capital, with DASH Ventures, FENA Holdings, and strategic angel investors anchoring the round. The funding will accelerate product development and global expansion. Breez enables businesses to launch real-time Voice AI in minutes, without code, by unifying global routing, uptime, and latency into reliable phone workflows and addressing the fragmented infrastructure behind phone-based operations

metadata.title: Wamda Capital leads Breez AI in $1.3 million pre-seed round

text:
Titre:
Wamda Capital leads Breez AI in $1.3 million pre-seed round


Résumé:
Breez AI has raised $1.3 million in pre-seed funding to scale its voice infrastructure system.


Startups mentionnées:
Breez AI


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Wamda Capital


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Morocco


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
. The company has already begun paid proofs of concept designed for thousands of daily calls and is partnering with AI and contact-center providers to run deployments across North America and the GCC, where rising call volumes are pushing today' s voice stack to its limits. Voice breaks because it was never designed to operate like software. Every phone call depends on dozens of real-time decisions happening simultaneously - routing, media delivery, AI processing, and carrier coordination - all within milliseconds. Most businesses attempt to assemble this from disconnected vendors and regional phone networks that were built for human call centers, not real-time automation

metadata.title: Wamda Capital leads Breez AI in $1.3 million pre-seed round

text:
Titre:
Wamda Capital leads Breez AI in $1.3 million pre-seed round


Résumé:
Breez AI has raised $1.3 million in pre-seed funding to scale its voice infrastructure system.


Startups mentionnées:
Breez AI


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Wamda Capital


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Morocco


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
. The result is fragile systems that fail under load: delayed responses, dropped calls, robotic interactions, and inconsistent performance across regions. It' s an infrastructure problem - and it' s the missing layer that has held voice automation back. Despite the rise of digital channels, voice remains the dominant service channel, accounting for around 55% of contact-center interactions. The global call-center market is valued at approximately $37.4 billion in 2025, reflecting continued reliance on phone operations (Plivo, 2025; Future Market Insights, 2025)

metadata.title: Wamda Capital leads Breez AI in $1.3 million pre-seed round

text:
Titre:
Wamda Capital leads Breez AI in $1.3 million pre-seed round


Résumé:
Breez AI has raised $1.3 million in pre-seed funding to scale its voice infrastructure system.


Startups mentionnées:
Breez AI


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Wamda Capital


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Morocco


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
. Breez replaces fragmented voice stacks with a single no-code orchestration layer, enabling organizations to deploy real-time Voice AI across their phone operations without integrating multiple systems. Businesses can launch almost instantly with natural-sounding, multilingual automation at scale, reducing delays, call failures, and missed opportunities - all without adding complex engineering or operational headcount."I started Breez because voice was the last manual bottleneck in digital operations," said Karim Malhas, Founder and CEO."What breaks voice isn' t the AI - it' s the systems around it

metadata.title: Wamda Capital leads Breez AI in $1.3 million pre-seed round

text:
Titre:
Wamda Capital leads Breez AI in $1.3 million pre-seed round


Résumé:
Breez AI has raised $1.3 million in pre-seed funding to scale its voice infrastructure system.


Startups mentionnées:
Breez AI


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Wamda Capital


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Morocco


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
. We built the missing layer: reliable calls at scale, with no developers and no stitching.""Voice never kept up with digital," Malhas added."Our goal is to make it as reliable as software - push a button, go live globally, and stay fast." The conversational AI market is expected to exceed $23 billion by 2027 as enterprises expand AI-driven customer interactions (Future CIO, 2024). Breez AI is a no-code voice orchestration platform built for reliable, scalable real-time Voice AI

metadata.title: Wamda Capital leads Breez AI in $1.3 million pre-seed round

text:
Titre:
Wamda Capital leads Breez AI in $1.3 million pre-seed round


Résumé:
Breez AI has raised $1.3 million in pre-seed funding to scale its voice infrastructure system.

Breez AI


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Wamda Capital

Article:
. Breez AI is a no-code voice orchestration platform built for reliable, scalable real-time Voice AI. The system unifies carrier connectivity, media delivery, AI models, and orchestration into one global routing layer, stabilizing phone operations and enabling companies to scale voice automation without engineering or additional headcount. Early users can join the Breez beta waitlist at heybreez., ai .

Article:
Moroccan recruitment startup Jobzyn has raised an undisclosed investment from Janngo Capital, one of Africa' s leading gender-lens tech investors. The funding will accelerate Jobzyn' s mission to transform recruitment across Africa through artificial intelligence. Founded in 2021 by CEO Simo Zizi, Jobzyn' s AI-powered platform tackles two major hiring challenges: helping employers filter large volumes of underqualified applications and guiding candidates toward better career choices

The platform automatically matches candidates to suitable roles, assesses soft skills before interviews, and provides employers with actionable insights such as salary benchmarks, workplace images, and leadership vision. Jobzyn already counts Deloitte, Pw C, Glovo, Maroc Telecom, Intelcia, Inwi, and TGCC among its clients and powers digital career centres for universities including INSEA, ESI, HEM, and HEC Rabat."With Janngo' s backing, we are accelerating our mission to make the job market more accessible, fair, and efficient across the continent," said Zizi. The company plans to use the funds to scale its AI-driven recruitment solutions and expand its footprint across Africa.

Article:
Dawar by Environ Adapt, the Cairo-based circular economy platform, has acquired a strategic stake in Bekya Pay, a consumer-facing application that enables households to exchange sorted recyclable materials for cash. The acquisition extends Dawar' s digital infrastructure beyond aggregation and trade layers to the point of waste generation. By integrating Bekya Pay, Dawar captures source-level data before materials enter informal trade networks, strengthening traceability across the recycling value chain

metadata.title: Dawar acquires stake in Bekya Pay to digitize Egypt' s recycling"first mile"

Founded to formalize fragmented waste recovery flows, Dawar operates as a digital infrastructure layer that records and verifies recyclable material movements across collection points, aggregators, and traders. Over the past three years, the company reports documenting more than 90,000 verified tons of recyclables across 22 governorates. Bekya Pay, launched less than a year ago, connects more than 30,000 users through a network of 500 collection points and 120 collectors across two governorates. The integration enhances early-stage data visibility, supporting compliance as Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) regulations and ESG reporting standards tighten

Tunisia-based venture capital firm 216 Capital has announced a strategic investment in Talenteo, an HR Tech startup founded in Algeria. Talenteo offers a comprehensive Saa S platform for human resources and payroll management tailored to the unique needs of African SMEs and mid-sized companies (ETIs). This investment marks a significant step in Talenteo' s journey toward becoming a key digital HR player across the continent. By 2030, Africa is projected to host more than 1 billion working-age individuals, making it the largest talent pool globally. With over 80% of jobs created by SMEs and ETIs, efficient HR and payroll management is critical for performance, compliance, and competitiveness

Article:
Egypt' s deep-tech and life science company specialized in scientific and industrial testing services, Nawah Scientific, has closed a $23 million Series A round (a mix of equity and debt) as it marks 10 years since launch. The round was led by Life Ventures Holding, with participation from Den Ventures, Empire M, Afric Invest, and Elsewedy, alongside banks and angel investors. Nawah says the capital will back three priorities: building a 10,000-sqm global R&D centre in Rwanda, doubling lab capacity in Egypt and Saudi Arabia, and expanding across North Africa and Europe. Founder and CEO Omar Shokry Saqr said the round strengthens Nawah' s research and tech backbone for its next growth phase

Article:
216 Capital announces a new strategic investment in Addvocate., AI, a Franco-Tunisian startup specialised in optimising sales performance with artificial intelligence. With the exponential rise of artificial intelligence, the sales sector is entering a new era of smart automation. Today, having a digital copilot capable of managing and optimising every lever of sales performance is no longer a dream-it is a reality made possible by Addvocate., AI, the startup redefining intelligent sales. Addvocate., AI, an innovative response to a structural challenge At the origin of Addvocate., AI is the field experience of Ridha Mami, Founder and CEO, who identified a major gap in supporting sales teams

. After more than fifteen years as a Sales Leader in B2B Saa S scale-ups, he kept witnessing the same scenario: countless hours wasted preparing for client meetings, compiling scattered information, and above all-seeing entire deals stall without any clear explanation, threatening business predictability. According to Mc Kinsey, between 35% and 50% of sales opportunities stagnate in pipelines, slowing company growth

This is how Addvocate., AI was born: a copilot designed to prepare salespeople for their meetings through AI and data consolidation."With the support of 216 Capital, we are equipped to stay ahead, breaking through the limits of traditional CRM and delivering a solution built for impact, not for data entry." Mami explains. Where Addvocate initially addressed the symptom (preparation), Nudge tackles sales performance before, during, and after meetings. With this approach, Addvocate., AI becomes a trusted partner for salespeople, making their meetings more productive, focused, and effective, while maximizing the chances of closing or quickly moving opportunities forward in the pipeline

Article:
Cairo-based e-commerce enablement platform Wuilt has raised $2 million in a funding round led by follow-on investment from Flat6Labs and MTF VC, with participation from Hub71, JIMCO (Abdul Latif Jameel' s VC arm), Purity Tech, and a group of strategic angel investors. The funding comes after Wuilt' s bold April 2025 pivot to offer its core platform completely free in Egypt, eliminating all subscription fees and forgoing hundreds of thousands of dollars in annual recurring revenue to accelerate merchant adoption. Since then, over 20,000 merchants have joined the platform

Titre:
Jordan' s Alefredo Ed Tech acquires UK's Tutor House in $600,000 deal


Résumé:
Alefredo Ed Tech acquires Tutor House in a $600,000 deal, expanding its reach in the UK education market.

Alefredo Ed Tech


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Sheraa


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Jordan


Secteurs:

Article:
Alefredo Ed Tech, a Jordan-based education technology startup, has acquired Tutor House, a UK-based online tutoring platform, in a transaction valued at $600,000. The deal is primarily structured as shares in Alefredo Holding Company, alongside additional strategic and performance-based considerations. Through the acquisition, Alefredo strengthens its presence in the UK education market while reinforcing Jordan' s position as a regional hub for education technology. Tutor House brings a network of more than 30,000 teachers serving over 15,000 students globally, significantly expanding Alefredo' s reach and capabilities in curriculum-aligned tutoring and live learning support

metadata.title: Jordan' s Alefredo Ed Tech acquires UK's Tutor House in $600,000 deal

Article:
. Founded in 2020 by Ahmad Al Saif, Alefredo began as a used-books marketplace before evolving into a provider of digital learning resources, aptitude tests, and interactive study tools for international curricula, including British Edexcel, A Level, O Level, Cambridge, IB, and AP systems. The company expanded into the UAE in 2022 through Sheraa and has served 20,000+ customers across 16 countries. According to Alefredo, the acquisition completes its end-to-end AI-enabled learning loop, integrating curriculum-native tutoring, live human support, assessments, analytics, and adaptive learning journeys

The transaction comes amid growing consolidation in the regional edtech sector, as Jordanian startups increasingly pursue cross-border acquisitions to scale globally.

Arab Fund invests in Shorooq' s Nahda Fund II to expand SME credit access in MENA

Article:
In a strategic move to bridge the financing gap for small and medium enterprises (SMEs) across the Arab world, the Arab Fund for Economic and Social Development , through its SME financing arm, Badir Fund, is investing in Shorooq' s Nahda Fund II, a Qualified Investor Fund registered with the FSRA ADGM. This investment aims to enhance access to credit for technology companies, fostering economic growth and innovation in the region. Badir Fund for Developing SMEs is a special account managed by the Arab Fund. It helps small businesses access the financing and technical assistance they need to grow and profit, creating jobs and driving private sector-led growth

Shorooq, regulated by the FSRA ADGM as a Fund Manager, is a leading alternative investment manager in the MENA region and has pioneered debt financing solutions for startups through its Nahda Funds. The second iteration, Nahda Fund II, continues this mission by offering flexible financing options to growing businesses, signifying a revitalisation of entrepreneurial ventures in the region. The collaboration between the Badir Fund and Shorooq' s Nahda Fund II comes at a time when SMEs face challenges in accessing traditional bank financing. Banks often have restrictive lending criteria, leaving many SMEs underserved

Article:
Karm (also known as Karm Solar), Egypt' s pioneering solar energy utility, announced today its official expansion into the Cypriot energy market with the establishment of Karm Cyprus, the company' s first international venture. As part of this expansion, Karm Cyprus has secured a license to build and operate a 7.6 MW solar PV plant in Monagroulli, located in southern Cyprus, and expected to be operational by September 2026. In September 2025, Karm Cyprus finalized EUR 5 million in project financing from Euro Bank (formerly Hellenic Bank) to support the PV plant construction

Article:
Qme, an emerging B2B SaaS startup based in Egypt, has raised $3 million in a seed funding round led by AHOY, a multisector technology company, along with a group of angel investors from the GCC. Qme is an AI-driven platform addressing a critical issue in MENA, where inefficient queuing systems and outdated appointment booking methods cause individuals to lose an average of six months of their lives standing in line. Moreover, the reliance on phone bookings, which account for 92% of appointments in key sectors, leads to a no-show rate of 31%, creating further inefficiencies

Article:
Egyptian fintech startup INVIA has raised $1.2 million from angel investors and strategic supporters to scale its AI-powered financial operating system for small and medium-sized enterprises. Founded to address the growing complexity of financial management for SMEs, INVIA replaces fragmented tools with a unified platform that automates bookkeeping, cash flow tracking, inventory, and manufacturing operations. The platform is designed for accessibility, allowing business owners to interact through simple inputs such as text, voice notes, or uploaded invoices, eliminating the need for traditional accounting expertise

Article:
MNT-Halan, Egypt's leading fintech ecosystem, has reached a valuation of $1.4 billion following the first closing of a new investment round led by Al Ahly Capital, the investment arm of the National Bank of Egypt. The transaction was completed following the receipt of all required regulatory approvals, with a second closing expected as part of the ongoing funding round. The investment reflects growing confidence in Egypt's fintech sector and highlights increasing collaboration between traditional financial institutions and technology-driven financial services providers. It also reinforces MNT-Halan's position as one of the region's largest fintech platforms

The programme introduces a structured venture-building approach, combining financial support with hands-on operational guidance to support founders across the full startup lifecycle, from ideation to market expansion. Article:
Digified is proud to announce that it has secured regulatory approval from the Financial Regulatory Authority (FRA) for its AI-powered digital onboarding, e KYC, and e-contracting services tailored for non-banking financial institutions (NBFIs) in Egypt. With this regulatory milestone, lending, investment, and insurance companies regulated by the FRA can now fully create digital IDs, verify their e-kyc, and sign legally binding contracts - all without paperwork, wet signatures, or physical presence. These digital contracts carry full legal weight in Egyptian courts, enabling fintech apps to function as official digital branches

Résumé:
Egypt' s digital SME lending platform Flend has announced the successful closure of its $3 million seed funding round.

Flend

Aucune


Pays:
Egypt


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
Egypt' s digital SME lending platform Flend has announced the successful closure of its $3 million seed funding round, a mix of equity and debt. The equity round was led by Egypt Ventures, with participation from Camel Ventures, Sukna Ventures, Plus VC, Banque Misr, and prominent family offices including El Sewedy and Baalbaki. On the debt side, Flend secured funding from MSMEDA and several local banking partners. Licensed by Egypt' s Financial Regulatory Authority (FRA) as a Digital Non-Banking Financial Institution (Digital NBFI), Flend enables fully digital lending for SMEs, from onboarding and credit scoring to disbursement and collections, all through digitally binding contracts

Article:
Taager, the Egyptian social e-commerce platform, has officially expanded into Morocco, opening operations in Casablanca as part of its wider regional growth across MENA. This move marks Taager' s first entry into North Africa after establishing its presence in Egypt, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE. The expansion targets Morocco' s fast-developing e-commerce sector, where Taager aims to make online selling more accessible to young, digital-first entrepreneurs. Founded in 2019, Taager offers an end-to-end platform that allows individuals to start selling online without carrying inventory or paying any upfront costs

Taager' s Moroccan operations will be led by Salma Ammor, who brings experience in scaling digital ventures. She aims to focus on providing Morocco' s youth with the tools they need to accelerate their businesses. Taager' s entry into Morocco reflects the rising regional demand for low-barrier e-commerce tools

Article:
VOLZ, the pioneering Algerian travel-tech startup and the first local platform enabling travellers to book flights in dinars with online or cash-ondelivery payment, announces a historic funding round of 600 million dinars (nearly $5 million). The round was led by a consortium of private investors under the leadership of Tell Group, with the participation of Groupe GIBA - Babahoum Industrial Group Algeria, known notably for its mineral water brand Guedila. Article:
A Ventures is an Egypt-based investment and portfolio management firm founded in 2024 and led by Sherif Ramadan and Ayman Abbas. A Ventures, a leading investment and portfolio management firm led by Sherif Ramadan (CEO) and Ayman Abbas (Chairman), has reinforced its commitment to Mrkoon, a pioneering digital marketplace for scrap and surplus materials, by following up its initial pre-seed investment with a new bridge round. This latest investment underscores A Ventures' confidence in Mrkoon' s exponential growth, operational impact, and its potential to transform a traditionally fragmented industry

Founded by Mohamed Shalabi (CEO), Ahmed Mamdouh (COO), and Ahmed Amir (CTO), Mrkoon is disrupting an industry worth billions in the MENA region with its tech-driven marketplace

Disrup Tech Ventures invests in Nigerian startup Winich Farms, a fast-growing Agri-Tech that addresses market fragmentation and lack of financial inclusion for smallholder farmers.

Article:
Disrup Tech Ventures Backs Nigerian Agri-Fin Tech Startup Winich Farms, Marking Its First Investment in Sub-Saharan Africa Disrup Tech Ventures, one of Egypt' s leading early-stage fintech funds, has announced its first pan-African investment in Nigerian startup Winich Farms, a fast-growing Agri-Tech that is transforming access to markets and credit for smallholder farmers across Nigeria. The investment forms part of Winich' s Pre-Series A round. Lagos-based Winich is addressing the most pressing challenges facing Nigeria' s agriculture sector, market fragmentation and lack of financial inclusion

metadata.title: Egypt' s Disrup Tech backs Nigerian agri-fintech Winich Farms

. Despite agriculture contributing 21% of Nigeria' s GDP and employing millions, smallholder farmers-who make up 80% of the farming population and produce 90% of output-remain largely excluded from modern supply chains and financial systems. Commenting on their debut international investment, Mohamed Okasha, Managing Partner at Disrup Tech Ventures, stated:"Our investment in Winich reflects our conviction in the potential of Nigeria' s agri-fintech sector and the scalability of its model. Winich is not only solving real problems for smallholder farmers but doing so with a scalable model

Their experience in scaling early-stage fintechs will be invaluable as we strengthen our operations, empower more farmers, and explore expansion opportunities across Africa and beyond

Article:
. In addition, Winich provides direct credit and agronomic advisory services, in partnership with Kebbi Agricultural Research Development Agency (KARDA), to help farmers scale operations and improve productivity. Looking ahead, Winich plans to leverage its success in Nigeria to scale across other African markets and explore export partnerships into the MENA region. With rising demand for reliable, traceable, and tech-enabled agricultural supply chains, Winich is well-positioned to become a continental leader in post-harvest agri-fintech solutions.

metadata.title: Egypt' s Disrup Tech backs Nigerian agri-fintech Winich Farms

Article:
Business For Teens, an education startup focused on teaching entrepreneurship and financial literacy to teenagers, has announced the successful closure of a six-figure pre-seed funding round to support its growth and expansion across Egypt and the Gulf region. The funding round comes on the back of strong early traction. Since its launch, Business For Teens has partnered with more than 10 schools across Egypt and Saudi Arabia, reaching over 600 students aged 10-16 through hands-on entrepreneurship programs

The funding marks a significant milestone in Zabon Ex' s mission to help F&B companies sell more and waste less through smart, data-driven solutions. text:
Titre:
Egypt' s Reme-D secures GIF backing to cut diagnostic costs by 40%


Résumé:
Egyptian biotech startup Reme-D secures $500,000 investment from Global Innovation Fund (GIF) to expand operations and scale accessible molecular diagnostics across Africa and the Middle East.


Startups mentionnées:
Reme-D


Investisseurs mentionnés:
Global Innovation Fund (GIF)


Fonds mentionnés:
Aucune


Pays:
Egypt


Secteurs:
Aucun


Article:
Egyptian biotech startup Reme-D has secured a $500,000 investment from the Global Innovation Fund (GIF) to expand its operations and scale accessible molecular diagnostics across Africa and the Middle East. Founded in 2022, Reme-D develops PCR testing kits that remain stable at room temperature, eliminating the need for cold-chain logistics. By leveraging nanotechnology and lyophilisation techniques, the company enables cost-effective and reliable diagnostics in infrastructure-constrained environments. The technology reduces operational costs by up to 40% while minimising reagent waste and improving testing reliability, particularly in regions with limited access to consistent electricity

metadata.title: Egypt' s Reme-D secures GIF backing to cut diagnostic costs by 40%
